Perhaps once more nodes appear in my area, things should improve.

It doesn't help that I'm the only MC user in my area as Meshtastic seems to be more popular here, but I got fed up with only 25% of my messages getting out and thought I'd give MC a try. Perhaps once more nodes appear in my area, things should improve.

messages.author: Eccles
messages.text: Unlike meshtastic, the companion nodes don't relay, so you need line of sight to a repeater, which from your earlier messages with Dayle puts you on the fringe. We could do with some more well positioned relays around the area. I'm trying to scout some areas when I have time. Though I'm about to go on a week's hiatus
messages.content_html: Unlike meshtastic, the companion nodes don't relay, so you need line of sight to a repeater, which from your earlier messages with Dayle puts you on the fringe. We could do with some more well positioned relays around the area. I'm trying to scout some areas when I have time. Though I'm about to go on a week's hiatus

I checked the SWR for 868mhz, it was low mainly because of losses in the coax. I couldn't 'recommend' it but it might be worth a try. These little dev boards put out so little power it's hard to kill them.

messages.author: SteBurneyM41-DDC
messages.text: Im struggling a bit as to what to bo bud. Its a G2 and its the most difficult device to get right. I changed feeder as you know earlier today. I lowered the power significantly yet it wouldnt even complete a trace route. I kept going up in 1dm incraments. The only time it started behaving was at about 1.5w. Bare in mind im forced to use 5m of feeder too. Its going to take lots of tweeking but im aiming to get is obviously rx and tx the same. Its a shame with this lark you cant just change a setting and get the results, it takes ages as you know pal

messages.author: SteBurneyM41-DDC
messages.text: Its early days pal, like i say. It can take ages getting it right. I totally get if i can only recieve 1.2w theres no point in transmitting at anymore than 1.2, but there is some sort of complicated correlation between the tx amp and the rx amp i dont fully understand, its burning my head out. Hopefully i will find the sweet spot very soon. I emailed Niel Hao regarding tweeking the rx too in the software but its not possible.
messages.content_html: Its early days pal, like i say. It can take ages getting it right. I totally get if i can only recieve 1.2w theres no point in transmitting at anymore than 1.2, but there is some sort of complicated correlation between the tx amp and the rx amp i dont fully understand, its burning my head out. Hopefully i will find the sweet spot very soon. I emailed Niel Hao regarding tweeking the rx too in the software but its not possible.
messages.reply_html:
G5TAR - Mike
Thanks Ste, really appreciate you trying to dial it in. Obviously don’t do anything that’s going to have a detrimental effect for you guys. If we start getting issues with congestion / collisions on our side I can just disable HX2

messages.author: drewzh
messages.text: I'm going to add a laminated card to my remote but rather public node (courtesy of GPT) Hello! This small device is part of a community wireless project called MeshCore. It helps provide free, low-power long-range connectivity for sensors and messages in the local area. I’ve placed it here temporarily because the location is ideal for coverage. It doesn’t damage the land, doesn’t use much power (less than a mobile phone charger), and is safe for people, animals, and the environment. If you are the landowner, I’d really like to hear from you. If you’d prefer I remove it, please just let me know and I’ll do so right away. If you’re curious, I’d also be happy to explain more about the project — you may even find it useful yourself! Please feel free to drop me a quick message at: [your email / anonymous address] Thank you for your understanding, A local MeshCore community member
messages.content_html: I'm going to add a laminated card to my remote but rather public node (courtesy of GPT) Hello! This small device is part of a community wireless project called MeshCore. It helps provide free, low-power long-range connectivity for sensors and messages in the local area. I’ve placed it here temporarily because the location is ideal for coverage. It doesn’t damage the land, doesn’t use much power (less than a mobile phone charger), and is safe for people, animals, and the environment. If you are the landowner, I’d really like to hear from you. If you’d prefer I remove it, please just let me know and I’ll do so right away. If you’re curious, I’d also be happy to explain more about the project — you may even find it useful yourself! Please feel free to drop me a quick message at: [your email / anonymous address] Thank you for your understanding, A local MeshCore community member

messages.author: drewzh
messages.text: Nice. Few tips (I've been building/hacking outdoor things for a while now so hope you don't mind) - run your self sealing tape from the bottom up, it creates an overlapping joint that sheds water, similar to tiles on a roof. Also, you'd be surprised how much dirt will collect on a flat mounted panel like that (I assume you're in between houses which is why it's angled up) - it's really best to angle it roughly 40 degrees if you can, or at least angled slightly, as it will not only have significant gains in power absorption, but it'll keep it much cleaner long term.
messages.content_html: Nice. Few tips (I've been building/hacking outdoor things for a while now so hope you don't mind) - run your self sealing tape from the bottom up, it creates an overlapping joint that sheds water, similar to tiles on a roof. Also, you'd be surprised how much dirt will collect on a flat mounted panel like that (I assume you're in between houses which is why it's angled up) - it's really best to angle it roughly 40 degrees if you can, or at least angled slightly, as it will not only have significant gains in power absorption, but it'll keep it much cleaner long term.

messages.author: G5TAR - Mike
messages.text: @SteBurneyM41-DDC we saw your messages around stacking attenuators on the G2 - isn’t that just making the already poor Rx even worse? It’s not working either as we’re still receiving all the flood packets from you guys. Anyways I’ve had to disable HX2 tonight as so many of our guys were complaining - which has resulted in some of our South Yorkshire guys becoming isolated from the mesh. HX2 (the repeater you guys are flooding) is a remote solar repeater running legal power, a 40 minute drive away from where I live, on private farmland where I got the land owner to agree to host the node. There’s nothing i can physically do to try and prevent your traffic bridging across as I’ll just end up winding the land owner up to the point where he makes me take the node down. So I’ve just had to switch it off
messages.content_html: @SteBurneyM41-DDC we saw your messages around stacking attenuators on the G2 - isn’t that just making the already poor Rx even worse? It’s not working either as we’re still receiving all the flood packets from you guys. Anyways I’ve had to disable HX2 tonight as so many of our guys were complaining - which has resulted in some of our South Yorkshire guys becoming isolated from the mesh. HX2 (the repeater you guys are flooding) is a remote solar repeater running legal power, a 40 minute drive away from where I live, on private farmland where I got the land owner to agree to host the node. There’s nothing i can physically do to try and prevent your traffic bridging across as I’ll just end up winding the land owner up to the point where he makes me take the node down. So I’ve just had to switch it off

messages.author: Mystic
messages.text: should certainly be possible - messages start with a header describing their type then a list of all repeater id it passed through so shouldn't be too difficult to have a list of disallowed repeater ids from which packets are dropped. you could theoretically put a hardcoded if (pkt->path_len != 0 && pkt->path[pkt->path_len - 1] == 0x02) { _mgr->free(pkt); return; } , but might be better to wait for it to be implemented as an actual feature (edited)

messages.author: drewzh
messages.text: From what I'm told and from some research into the coding rate - it's the TX side that decides the error correction to apply to the packet. So it wouldn't matter if I upped the CR on my side, as others are hearing my repeater loud and clear (including winter hill). Where I fall short is hearing WH... which means the only way CR would help me is if they upped theres.... but as I said in my first message, that's obviously not what we want, so I'm looking into my noise floor (edited)

messages.text: if i do have to ask chatgpt for something, i tend to phrase it neutrally rather than asking it if it agrees with my side. e.g., in this case, something like 'what does increasing the LoRa CR do in the context of a repeater on meshcore/meshtastic'. otherwise, its more likely to agree with what you're proposing

messages.author: drewzh
messages.text: Nice - I used to live in bury for a few years (tottington road + rochdale road). What is your local repeater? Winter hill? It probably doesn't matter much if you're getting good SNR from the repeater, but ideally if you can move it away from the wood, that would be ideal (it'll soak up some radiation but not as much as metal). What I've done is screw a small piece of timber to the joist and then mount the antenna to the top of that.

It probably doesn't matter much if you're getting good SNR from the repeater, but ideally if you can move it away from the wood, that would be ideal (it'll soak up some radiation but not as much as metal). messages.author: Yisarus
messages.text: Best way to see if there are any repeaters in line of sight is to use the web app (you don't need a companion connected) click on the 3 dots to the top right and click on Tools. Then select Line of Sight, From there you can find where you are on the map, left click and hold to place a marker for your repeater, then do the same for locations of other repeaters to drop another marker. This will then do a line of sight calculation and show you if any terrain is blocking the path. https://app.meshcore.nz/ (edited)
messages.content_html: Best way to see if there are any repeaters in line of sight is to use the web app (you don't need a companion connected) click on the 3 dots to the top right and click on Tools. Then select Line of Sight, From there you can find where you are on the map, left click and hold to place a marker for your repeater, then do the same for locations of other repeaters to drop another marker. This will then do a line of sight calculation and show you if any terrain is blocking the path. https://app.meshcore.nz/ (edited)

messages.author: Tharsis
messages.text: Just want to sanity check myself. I know I'm not exactly in the zone of this region (I'm in the Chorley area), but was wondering if anyone can see any repeaters near Chorley/Preston? I set up a MeshCore node (And a Meshtastic one), and while the MT node has found a good 60/70 nodes stretching from Lancaster to North Wales and plenty in Manchester, the MC node hasn't found a single other node. I'm currently configured using the EU/UK Narrow present after recently discovering that everyone in the UK has apparently switched to that.
messages.content_html: Just want to sanity check myself. I know I'm not exactly in the zone of this region (I'm in the Chorley area), but was wondering if anyone can see any repeaters near Chorley/Preston? I set up a MeshCore node (And a Meshtastic one), and while the MT node has found a good 60/70 nodes stretching from Lancaster to North Wales and plenty in Manchester, the MC node hasn't found a single other node. I'm currently configured using the EU/UK Narrow present after recently discovering that everyone in the UK has apparently switched to that.

messages.author: Tharsis
messages.text: Thanks for the info. I suspected that might be the case. Going by the MeshCore map, most nearby nodes were last seen back in July (And most earlier), so I had no idea if they were still live or not. I'll keep my node live for the foreseeable future regardless, and maybe some of the MT guys near me might add MC nodes as it continues to pick up momentum. I'll also keep an eye out for your Welsh repeater, as my MT node is definitely picking up more to the west of me and I've gotten good reception for Liverpool and north Wales. (edited)

messages.author: likkleidiot_nwo
messages.text: Just been doing some power test at ideal for the Heltec V3, Xiao Esp32 S3 and Xiao nRF52840 All running Meshcore Bluetooth companion. I know the V3 is power hungry but didn't realise just how much. The Xiao esp32 S3 and the V3 both use the same chips but vast difference in power usage. Be interesting to test the V4 once it arrives This is based on a 5000mah lipo for the estimated runtimes.Test were done with USB for power consumption Inputs used (idle): Heltec V3: 4.761 V, 0.612 A → 2.916 W XIAO ESP32-S3: 4.947 V, 0.108 A → 0.537 W XIAO nRF52840: 4.980 V, 0.017 A → 0.084 W Battery-only runtime (ideal): Heltec V3: 6.34 h XIAO ESP32-S3: 34.45 h XIAO nRF52840: 220.24 h (≈9.18 days) (edited)
messages.content_html: Just been doing some power test at ideal for the Heltec V3, Xiao Esp32 S3 and Xiao nRF52840 All running Meshcore Bluetooth companion. I know the V3 is power hungry but didn't realise just how much. The Xiao esp32 S3 and the V3 both use the same chips but vast difference in power usage. Be interesting to test the V4 once it arrives This is based on a 5000mah lipo for the estimated runtimes.Test were done with USB for power consumption Inputs used (idle): Heltec V3: 4.761 V, 0.612 A → 2.916 W XIAO ESP32-S3: 4.947 V, 0.108 A → 0.537 W XIAO nRF52840: 4.980 V, 0.017 A → 0.084 W Battery-only runtime (ideal): Heltec V3: 6.34 h XIAO ESP32-S3: 34.45 h XIAO nRF52840: 220.24 h (≈9.18 days) (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: likkleidiot_nwo
messages.text: Update on the V3 power usage at idle.. It's not as bad as I first reported. Looks like when powering via the USB the reported power usage is massive. Powering via battery drops it vastly .I'm new to all this so forgive me if I'm repeating old news. The other boards via usb didn't report this high value like V3. So the runtime on 5000mah battery with 5w 5v solar panel is estimated at 4.2 days. The better news is when I test the V3 in my crows nest carrier board it's more efficient running via battery on there than direct to the board..I'm assuming my charge circuit is a little more efficient. Again totally new to this so any pointers for power testing just let me know

messages.author: Bumblebee
messages.text: Who are the MT people and where are they getting nodes from in the west of ireland. There does not seem to be many people in Ireland using Meshcore, am up in Donegal and see no one, I have a Meshtastic node up and I see no other nodes, would like to get some other interested but they seem too wrapped up in themselves to even see the benefit of having Meshcore up and running, we had a bad stom a few months back, power off for 4 days, and phones out too. I just started a server / Mesh Networks Ireland https://discord.gg/sPUpWzds , I did because a guy started a website / meshcore.ie but it is unreachable, and he has not been on here in a while, think he gave up. Either way it is frustrating to have no other users of either mesh in my area, and am not in a good place, too low down, which means I need others involved if am ever to get outside my area.

messages.author: Frazzled
messages.text: The XIAO nRF has battery pads on the bottom of the board. So you know if these can be used to send the battery voltage whilst still powering it via the USB?
messages.content_html: The XIAO nRF has battery pads on the bottom of the board. So you know if these can be used to send the battery voltage whilst still powering it via the USB?

messages.text: When it's on a solar panel and it's not been sunny enough

messages.author: Frazzled
messages.text: WaveShare D

messages.author: Frazzled
messages.text: Will the XIAO just stop operating if it gets too low? Could I just connect the relevant bits of a USB cable to the battery terminals on the charger controller and then plug that into the USB on the XIAO? At the moment the charger controller just stopped powering the XIAO when it gets down to 3.0v, so I would potentially lose that protection by going direct to the battery.
messages.content_html: Will the XIAO just stop operating if it gets too low? Could I just connect the relevant bits of a USB cable to the battery terminals on the charger controller and then plug that into the USB on the XIAO? At the moment the charger controller just stopped powering the XIAO when it gets down to 3.0v, so I would potentially lose that protection by going direct to the battery.

messages.author: Frazzled
messages.text: Thanks Dayle! Much appreciated
messages.content_html: Thanks Dayle! Much appreciated
messages.reply_html:
Dayle - M0OUE
Because you have the waveshare module it changes things. If you had a battery on the battery terminals it would start to brown out when it transmit. The waveshare module has battery protection in place to stop it from discharging the battery too much. I wouldn't connect the battery terminals to usb. You could connect the battery terminals to the battery pads, that would mean that it could still draw from the battery. BUT 1. Make sure you don't use the usb on the xiao. 2. Make sure your batteries have a built in battery protection circuit. Also, is your node outside in the "sun"? There's been more than enough recently to keep nodes topped up, if it can't keep up there may be something wrong.... The waveshare may not be designed for how low the power draw is, it may just be disconnecting because it doesn't know anything is connected. Similar things happen with power banks.

messages.author: ChickenWire
messages.text: Hi all, I'm new to meshcore and Discord so please be gentle! I'm interested in building a solar-powered repeater, so I'm keen to hear the "accumulated wisdom" from those that have done it before. Are there some particularly good resources you can point me at that detail some of the most successful designs (esp. for our local environment/weather/latitude)? Any info or experiences you can share much appreciated. My aim would be to build something robust and reliable for a permanent installation. It's mainly the solar panel and BMS / battery protection aspects that I'm not yet confident on.
messages.content_html: Hi all, I'm new to meshcore and Discord so please be gentle! I'm interested in building a solar-powered repeater, so I'm keen to hear the "accumulated wisdom" from those that have done it before. messages.author: ChickenWire
messages.text: Thanks Dayle, I’ll check out the Shoshine panels. I’ll need to do some more research on the detail of different battery protections, as I’m conscious of the fire risk of Lipo batteries, which could be a problem during hot dry summers in rural locations. Might investigate using a different and more stable battery chemistry. Is anyone else worrying about that?! (edited)
messages.content_html: Thanks Dayle, I’ll check out the Shoshine panels. I’ll need to do some more research on the detail of different battery protections, as I’m conscious of the fire risk of Lipo batteries, which could be a problem during hot dry summers in rural locations. Might investigate using a different and more stable battery chemistry. Is anyone else worrying about that?! (edited)
messages.reply_html:
Dayle - M0OUE
Hello! it's best to use a nRF52840 based board like the XAIO or the RAK . For solar panels I've had good experiences with Soshine from Aliexpress. RAK has a decent charge controller on board if you want something simple, but a bit pricey. otherwise you can get any old MPPT charge controller board, a XIAO nRF52840 and wire them together with a battery. There are a few battery protection boards out there and it's always worth adding one. tese are designed for the terminals on a pouch battery, and any decent battery should have one already (for example from The Pi Hut.

messages.author: ChickenWire
messages.text: Nice! Would be curious to see a detailed circuit diagram if you’re open to sharing it (but entirely understand if you’d prefer not to)
messages.content_html: Nice! Would be curious to see a detailed circuit diagram if you’re open to sharing it (but entirely understand if you’d prefer not to)
messages.reply_html:
Dayle - M0OUE
It's on my mind yes. so much so that I'm designing a PCB with the ability to use different battery chemistry such as LiFePO4. It also has other safety features such as a thermistor to stop charging at low and high temps (following the JEITA standard), and fuses for the 18650 batteries in case of internal shorts.

messages.author: likkleidiot_nwo
messages.text: Sensors don't seem to be the focus..It seems the focus is creating zones for regional packets so the country isn't flooded, which is needed..the success of long range links is the very issue that's causing lost packets and messages...got folk getting more repeaters to fix missed messages when in fact adding more repeaters will ultimately make it worse..lol...I understand it's a fluid thing but I got sidetracked trying to help make the Mesh bigger which ultimately has made it a bit clunker...it will sort it's self out I'm sure but need to focus on my local mesh..I've not moved to Meshtastic yet .it was a convenience thing really not a choice. Just to prove my POC..been looking at Meshcore sensor build and moving to another frequency so not swamped with main Narrow traffic.

messages.author: drewzh
messages.text: I hear you on the sensors thing - it's just too early in the project for it to be a priority for them I guess. It will come in time though I'm sure - even with meshtastic is took a while to get decent support and even then, the issues plauging meshtastic always meant sensor reliability was always a problem. For instance I had a temperature and humidity sensor broadcasting from my hill top node every 3 hours and I rarely got a ping from it... maybe like once a day if I was lucky I have a lot of experience with Meshtastic (been using it for 2+ years). I've not had great success at all, even with a repeater in a similar position to my Dark Peak repeater. That said, for local small meshes I imagine it works quite well - I just didn't have anyone local to me at the time to really put that to the test. In regards to not receiving these wider broadcasts and keeping it local - are you aware of the feature that's in ideation to create a rudamentary firewall so disallow messages from different repeater etc - couldn't something like this be leveraged to improve your local only mesh? I'm interested to know how you get on with Meshtastic - just don't jump ship just yet until you've actually compared them side by side, because all of the talk of unreliability with Meshtastic isn't unfounded and it's the very reason why many of us have moved.

That said, for local small meshes I imagine it works quite well - I just didn't have anyone local to me at the time to really put that to the test. In regards to not receiving these wider broadcasts and keeping it local - are you aware of the feature that's in ideation to create a rudamentary firewall so disallow messages from different repeater etc - couldn't something like this be leveraged to improve your local only mesh? I'm interested to know how you get on with Meshtastic - just don't jump ship just yet until you've actually compared them side by side, because all of the talk of unreliability with Meshtastic isn't unfounded and it's the very reason why many of us have moved.
messages.reply_html:
likkleidiot_nwo
Sensors don't seem to be the focus..It seems the focus is creating zones for regional packets so the country isn't flooded, which is needed..the success of long range links is the very issue that's causing lost packets and messages...got folk getting more repeaters to fix missed messages when in fact adding more repeaters will ultimately make it worse..lol...I understand it's a fluid thing but I got sidetracked trying to help make the Mesh bigger which ultimately has made it a bit clunker...it will sort it's self out I'm sure but need to focus on my local mesh..I've not moved to Meshtastic yet .it was a convenience thing really not a choice. Just to prove my POC..been looking at Meshcore sensor build and moving to another frequency so not swamped with main Narrow traffic.

It will come in time though I'm sure - even with meshtastic is took a while to get decent support and even then, the issues plauging meshtastic always meant sensor reliability was always a problem. For instance I had a temperature and humidity sensor broadcasting from my hill top node every 3 hours and I rarely got a ping from it... maybe like once a day if I was lucky I have a lot of experience with Meshtastic (been using it for 2+ years). I've not had great success at all, even with a repeater in a similar position to my Dark Peak repeater. That said, for local small meshes I imagine it works quite well - I just didn't have anyone local to me at the time to really put that to the test. messages.author: tin
messages.text: I'm seeing tour disdain for the chat, and want to persuade you to keep going. I feel the same, I've started to feel I've invested nearly £1k in a tiny part of facilitating a crap public chat room - but I've remembered that was never the goal and it will iron out after a while. A mass effect was needed first and MC is definitely achieving that. THEN we can work out what to do to make nice uses out of it. Regard the many repeaters.... I do wonder.... I have definitely done that to make the comms into my area reliable. Two sites each with yagis NE and SW to hear distance, and an omni for stuff that's actually in the area. I'm starting to think about how to make each act as one and not go too mental with the rpts. I'd REALLY like to see real stats on how often actual MC repeats are causing issues with other MC repeats. Cos if it's really at some kind of breaking point, i'm interested how to start making it more reliable in other ways. (edited)

messages.author: Aegishjalmur
messages.text: Looks similar to ATAK; with the intent being search and rescue coordination here, can it be misued; absolutely; but there are far simpler methods and data which can already be missused for those purposes which are nothing new to meshcore nor meshtastic; look at the bluetooth specification as an example whereby you can locate any bluetooth device using the Received SIgnal Stength Indicator (RSSI) to wihtin centimeters of accuracy (this is why airtag, tile, etc are a thing) for example.

messages.author: M29
messages.text: Keep antenna connected before powering up like you say. I personally find keeping the antenna fully extended rather than a L shape gave me better SWR. Also if you don't have a case you could put a small hole on the lid of the box and a small hole on the left for the antenna and power. Won't look pretty like but keeps it a bit more secure and antenna vertical. (edited)

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: Just a heads-up – I’ve been tinkering with something called MeshRank It’s basically a way of giving a bit more visibility into what the mesh is actually doing, without changing how MeshCORE works. No internet routing, no messing with RF, nothing in the message path. It just listens, observes, and feeds back what already happened. Why I built it: • New repeaters often get switched off because it’s hard to tell if they’re helping • People end up spamming test messages just to see if anything’s alive • When stuff doesn’t propagate, there’s no clue why MeshRank gives things like delivery confidence, shows which repeaters are contributing, and highlights where messages are being seen but not going any further. Observers are totally passive — they don’t transmit or interfere in any way. If MeshRank fell over tomorrow, the mesh would carry on exactly the same. I’m looking for a few folks (UK first) who wouldn’t mind running an observer and helping build some early coverage — high ground / urban spots especially useful. If you fancy a look: https://meshrank.net Happy to answer questions or take criticism
messages.content_html: Just a heads-up – I’ve been tinkering with something called MeshRank It’s basically a way of giving a bit more visibility into what the mesh is actually doing, without changing how MeshCORE works. Why I built it: • New repeaters often get switched off because it’s hard to tell if they’re helping • People end up spamming test messages just to see if anything’s alive • When stuff doesn’t propagate, there’s no clue why MeshRank gives things like delivery confidence, shows which repeaters are contributing, and highlights where messages are being seen but not going any further. Observers are totally passive — they don’t transmit or interfere in any way. If MeshRank fell over tomorrow, the mesh would carry on exactly the same. messages.author: ChickenWire
messages.text: I don’t know if this is useful info for other folk considering solar repeaters vs plain battery powered, but I’ve been running a RAK-module based repeater (RAK plus a cheap 12V to USB converter) off a 12V 22Ah LifePO4 battery. messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)
messages.content_html: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)

messages.author: andyman5002
messages.text: Why isn’t the full hash used
messages.content_html: Why isn’t the full hash used

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: I expect it’s to keep the data packet lightweight. Up to 64 hops can be used. I believe they’ve thought about changing it. One more digit would reduce the chance of a duplicate significantly. I think if they built the mesh again they’d change a lot. This is my best way to fix it. I have introduced Vertibi and MHH modelling into my routing algorithm. The using the observers as ‘votes’ with some real world fine tuning in getting around 70% accuracy. If those in the worst affected areas changed their pub we would get to 85% In real world that’s about as accurate as you need for a tool to show roughly where you’re getting out.
messages.content_html: I expect it’s to keep the data packet lightweight. Up to 64 hops can be used. I believe they’ve thought about changing it. One more digit would reduce the chance of a duplicate significantly. I think if they built the mesh again they’d change a lot. This is my best way to fix it. I have introduced Vertibi and MHH modelling into my routing algorithm. The using the observers as ‘votes’ with some real world fine tuning in getting around 70% accuracy. If those in the worst affected areas changed their pub we would get to 85% In real world that’s about as accurate as you need for a tool to show roughly where you’re getting out.

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: I've been working on an idea for Meshrank.net to add a bot rank. useful bots in specialist groups can work, repetitive auto responders in test dont. so i was hoping to discourage the latter. (edited)
messages.content_html: I've been working on an idea for Meshrank.net to add a bot rank. useful bots in specialist groups can work, repetitive auto responders in test dont. so i was hoping to discourage the latter. (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 2
messages.reply_html:
Dayle - M0OUE
100%, tools like https://analyzer.letsmesh.net/channels negate the need for bots

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: There is definitely something I can do to help reduce bot activity using MeshRank. I’ve been thinking a lot about this. I do see 2 sides to the coin. Good bots in specialist channels can add valuable and useful info. Something that is not really used yet is how bots could help to relay emergency messages. I see them used well in weather channels. I also see the bad bots - the worst being autoresponders in #public. My honest take is I don’t think anyone has set up a bot to be malicious. Mainly amateurs trying to experiment and make something useful. Not realising the negative effect it’s causing. So by educating users I hope it may help. Here’s what I can do to help 1, create a bot rank to show good and bad activity and score bots on their behaviour (this will definitely get me some grief) so help around what determines good and bad is welcome. When I added a measure of Adverts I monitored and seen an almost immediate and noticeable reduction in advert traffic in the UK. 2, Create a free public api to show when traffic is high and low, bot builders can add this into their code so that bots don’t send when activity is high 3, Demonstrate and showcase good bot activity, which will encourage others to start to think outside the box.
messages.content_html: There is definitely something I can do to help reduce bot activity using MeshRank. I’ve been thinking a lot about this. I do see 2 sides to the coin. Good bots in specialist channels can add valuable and useful info. Something that is not really used yet is how bots could help to relay emergency messages. I see them used well in weather channels. I also see the bad bots - the worst being autoresponders in #public. My honest take is I don’t think anyone has set up a bot to be malicious. Mainly amateurs trying to experiment and make something useful. Not realising the negative effect it’s causing. So by educating users I hope it may help. Here’s what I can do to help 1, create a bot rank to show good and bad activity and score bots on their behaviour (this will definitely get me some grief) so help around what determines good and bad is welcome. When I added a measure of Adverts I monitored and seen an almost immediate and noticeable reduction in advert traffic in the UK. 2, Create a free public api to show when traffic is high and low, bot builders can add this into their code so that bots don’t send when activity is high 3, Demonstrate and showcase good bot activity, which will encourage others to start to think outside the box.
